subject: Mathematics
topic: Plan, Build, Grow — Math 8 Complete Coverage Add-On
grade_subject_fit: Grade 8 Math add-on for the Kamloops planter project using garden measurement, plant data, water amounts, and material cost decisions.
unit_context: Students have planter boxes, herbs, and seed pucks to observe. Each module uses the garden as a concrete context for Math 8 practice.
assignment_purpose: Patch Math 8 coverage gaps through one garden-based add-on package rather than disconnected worksheets.
final_evidence_target: Students make a short garden recommendation using measurement, data, cost, and design evidence.
student_task_flow: Use one visible math tool or formula.
Record a short calculation, table, graph, or comparison.
Explain one garden decision using numbers and units.
success_criteria: I can choose a math tool that fits the garden problem.
I can show calculation steps with units.
I can use garden data to support a recommendation.

supports_scaffolds.label: Reduced question load
supports_scaffolds.support: Each task sheet uses two or three larger garden problems instead of many small drills.

supports_scaffolds.label: Formula support
supports_scaffolds.support: Formula reminders and sentence frames are embedded in student-facing pages.

supports_scaffolds.label: Differentiated options
supports_scaffolds.support: Level A records and calculates with support; Level B completes the core task; Level C compares or justifies.

differentiation_model.support_pathway: Use pre-filled tables, formula boxes, calculators, and one-step garden numbers.
differentiation_model.core_pathway: Complete the main garden calculation and explain one decision using units.
differentiation_model.extension_pathway: Compare two options, justify a method, or create a second model using the same data.

checkpoint_release_logic: Check whether students can identify the correct unit before moving from measurement to area or volume.
Check whether students can match evidence to a garden decision before final recommendation writing.
teacher_implementation_notes: Use the real planter whenever possible.
Calculators are appropriate because the priority is setting up the math correctly.
Each module can run as one weekly math block with short daily garden interactions.
Assign one pathway per student or group rather than all extension work to everyone.
likely_misconceptions: Students may confuse length, area, and volume units.
Students may treat percent change as subtraction only.
Students may graph plant height without equal intervals.
Students may use Pythagorean theorem on non-right triangles.
pacing_shape: Eight weekly modules with slides, task sheets, checkpoints, exit tickets, organizers, and a final response.
assessment_focus: area, surface area, volume, and conversion reasoning
ratios, rates, proportional reasoning, and percents
algebra, equations, linear relations, and graphing
Pythagorean theorem, data analysis, probability, and financial literacy

lesson_overview.title: Plan, Build, Grow — Math 8 Complete Coverage Add-On
lesson_overview.overview: Students practise key Math 8 skills through planter dimensions, water, soil, plant growth, seed outcomes, graphing, cost, and design decisions.
lesson_overview.essential_question: How can Math 8 tools help us make better garden decisions in Kamloops?
lesson_overview.big_idea: Numbers, patterns, and data help us compare choices, check designs, and explain decisions in a real project.

sequence.focus: Measurement, units, perfect squares/cubes, and roots.
Area, surface area, and planter coverage.
Volume, soil, watering, and unit conversion.
Percents, germination, growth change, and budget.
Algebra, equations, input-output tables, graphing, and linear relations.
Data analysis, probability, cost comparison, and final recommendation.

lesson_overview.differentiation_note: Students work from the same garden context with supported, core, and extension pathways.
integrity_checks: Student tasks use garden or planter data.

teacher_guide.title: Teacher Guide — Math 8 Garden Complete Coverage Add-On
teacher_guide.big_idea: The planter project can carry broad Math 8 practice when each skill is tied to a visible garden decision.
learning_goals: Use perfect squares, perfect cubes, roots, and units in measurement contexts.
Calculate area, surface area, volume, and unit conversions for planter decisions.
Use ratios, rates, percents, and proportional reasoning with water, growth, seeds, and costs.
Represent plant growth using expressions, equations, tables, graphs, and linear relations.
Use Pythagorean theorem, data analysis, probability, and financial literacy in a final recommendation.
materials: Planter boxes or planter dimension cards
Tape measures, rulers, graph paper, and calculators

timing.activity: Observe plants, measure growth, check moisture, count seedlings, or record watering.
Launch with slides and one worked example.
Complete the task sheet or organizer with support.

teacher_notes: Keep the math visible and concrete.
Use the actual planter when possible.
Reduce number complexity while preserving the same concept.
The final response should be short and evidence-based.
integrity_checks: Student pages do not include teacher checkpoint notes.
Each module connects to a practical garden decision.

days.day_label: Module 1: Measurement, Units, Squares, Cubes, and Roots

content.subtitle: Use planter dimensions to review units, square numbers, cube numbers, and roots.

rows: Area uses square units like cm².
Volume uses cubic units like cm³.
Roots work backward from area or volume.

task_sheet.title: Module 1 Task — Garden Measurements and Roots
instructions: Use the planter or dimension card.

tasks.label: Measure
tasks.prompt: Record one planter dimension set. Length = ___ cm. Width = ___ cm. Height = ___ cm.

tasks.label: Squares and cubes
tasks.prompt: Circle perfect squares: 25, 30, 36, 42. Circle perfect cubes: 8, 12, 27, 32.

tasks.label: Work backward
tasks.prompt: A square tile has area 64 cm². Side length = √64 = ___ cm. A soil cube has volume 125 cm³. Edge length = ∛125 = ___ cm.

success_criteria: I identified perfect squares and cubes.

exit_ticket.title: Module 1 Exit Check
exit_ticket.prompt: Write one square unit and one cubic unit from the planter project.

days.day_label: Module 2: Area and Surface Area for Planter Coverage

content.subtitle: Calculate area and surface area for material decisions.

task_sheet.title: Module 2 Task — Area and Surface Coverage

tasks.label: Bottom area
tasks.prompt: Planter base: L = 100 cm, W = 40 cm. Bottom area = ___ × ___ = _____ cm².

tasks.label: Side coverage
tasks.prompt: One long side is 100 cm by 30 cm. One short side is 40 cm by 30 cm. Find both areas.

tasks.label: Coverage decision
tasks.prompt: Which surfaces need liner? Explain using one area calculation.

graphic_organizer.title: Planter Surface Area Organizer
graphic_organizer.prompt: Organize which planter surfaces need material.

checkpoint_sheet.title: Teacher Checkpoint — Area and Surface Area
checkpoint_sheet.checkpoint_focus: Check one-face area versus total surface coverage.
checkpoint_sheet.release_rule: Move on when students calculate one face area.

days.day_label: Module 3: Volume, Soil, Watering, and Unit Conversion

content.subtitle: Use volume and unit conversion to estimate soil and water.

task_sheet.title: Module 3 Task — Soil and Water Volume
instructions: Convert cm³ to litres by dividing by 1000.

tasks.label: Soil bags
tasks.prompt: One soil bag holds 25 L. Bags needed = litres ÷ 25 = ____. Round up to ____ bags.

tasks.label: Watering
tasks.prompt: Four plants each receive 250 mL. Total water = ___ mL = ___ L.

exit_ticket.title: Module 3 Exit Check
exit_ticket.prompt: Convert 1500 mL to litres and name where that unit appears in the garden.

days.day_label: Module 4: Ratios, Rates, and Proportional Reasoning

content.subtitle: Use ratios and rates to compare water, spacing, and planting plans.

task_sheet.title: Module 4 Task — Ratios, Rates, and Plant Spacing

tasks.label: Water rate
tasks.prompt: Three plants get 750 mL total. Water per plant = 750 ÷ 3 = ___ mL/plant. Six plants need ___ mL.

tasks.label: Spacing ratio
tasks.prompt: A plan uses 2 basil plants for every 3 lettuce plants. If we plant 4 basil, how many lettuce plants match the ratio?

tasks.label: Explain
tasks.prompt: Explain one watering or spacing decision using a rate or ratio.

graphic_organizer.title: Water and Plant Ratio Organizer
graphic_organizer.prompt: Use the organizer to keep the rate consistent.

checkpoint_sheet.title: Teacher Checkpoint — Ratios and Rates
checkpoint_sheet.checkpoint_focus: Check total water versus water per plant.
look_fors: Uses per in the unit rate
checkpoint_sheet.release_rule: Move on when students can find one unit rate.

days.day_label: Module 5: Percents, Germination, Growth Change, and Budget

content.subtitle: Use percent for seed germination, growth change, discount, tax, and budget.

task_sheet.title: Module 5 Task — Percent in the Garden

tasks.label: Germination percent
tasks.prompt: 18 out of 24 seeds sprouted. Percent sprouted = 18 ÷ 24 × 100 = ____%.

tasks.label: Growth percent
tasks.prompt: A basil plant grew from 8 cm to 10 cm. Change = ___ cm. Percent change = change ÷ original × 100 = ____%.

tasks.label: Budget
tasks.prompt: A soil bag costs $8.00. It is 25% off, then 12% tax is added. Estimate the final cost for 4 bags.

success_criteria: I calculated percent from garden data.

exit_ticket.title: Module 5 Exit Check
exit_ticket.prompt: If 15 out of 20 seeds sprout, what percent sprouted?

days.day_label: Module 6: Algebra, Equations, Linear Relations, and Graphing

content.subtitle: Use expressions, equations, tables, and graphs to model plant growth.

task_sheet.title: Module 6 Task — Growth Rules and Graphs

tasks.label: Expression
tasks.prompt: A plant starts at 6 cm and grows 2 cm per day. Expression: 6 + 2d. Find the height for d = 0, 1, 2, 3.

tasks.label: Two-step equation
tasks.prompt: Solve: 6 + 2d = 14. What does d mean in the garden?

tasks.label: Graph
tasks.prompt: Graph your table. x-axis = days. y-axis = height in cm. Write one sentence describing the trend.

success_criteria: I substituted values into an expression.
I solved a two-step equation with support.

checkpoint_sheet.title: Teacher Checkpoint — Algebra and Graphing
checkpoint_sheet.checkpoint_focus: Check input as days and output as height.
look_fors: Labels axes with days and cm
checkpoint_sheet.release_rule: Move to design checks when students can complete a small table.

days.day_label: Module 7: Pythagorean Theorem and Design Checks

content.subtitle: Use the Pythagorean theorem for diagonal and square-layout checks.

task_sheet.title: Module 7 Task — Pythagorean Design Check
instructions: Use the theorem only for right triangles.

tasks.label: Diagonal brace
tasks.prompt: A frame corner has legs 30 cm and 40 cm. 30² + 40² = c². c = √2500 = ___ cm.

tasks.label: Layout check
tasks.prompt: A bed has sides 60 cm and 80 cm. Estimate the diagonal using the same steps.

tasks.label: Explain
tasks.prompt: Why might a builder measure diagonals to check whether a rectangular planter is square?

exit_ticket.title: Module 7 Exit Check
exit_ticket.prompt: A right triangle has legs 6 cm and 8 cm. Find the diagonal length.
success_criteria: I found the square root at the end.

days.day_label: Module 8: Data Analysis, Probability, and Final Recommendation

content.subtitle: Use plant data, probability, and cost to make a final garden recommendation.

task_sheet.title: Module 8 Task — Data and Probability Summary
instructions: Use real class data if available.

tasks.label: Data summary
tasks.prompt: Plant heights: 6 cm, 8 cm, 8 cm, 10 cm, 13 cm. Find mean, median, mode, and range.

tasks.label: Probability
tasks.prompt: In one tray, 18 of 24 seeds sprouted. Write the probability as a fraction and percent if you can.

tasks.label: Cost comparison
tasks.prompt: Option A costs $36 for 12 plants. Option B costs $45 for 18 plants. Find cost per plant and choose the better value.

final_response_sheet.title: Final Garden Math Recommendation
final_response_sheet.prompt: Make a recommendation for the next garden decision. Use at least three numbers: one measurement or calculation, one data/probability result, and one cost or budget comparison.
planning_reminders: Use numbers from your task sheets.
Name the math tool you used.

frame_strip: The measurement/calculation that supports this is ___.
paragraph_support.reminder_box: A strong recommendation uses evidence from the garden, not just opinion.

success_criteria: I used at least three numbers.
I explained how the numbers support my decision.

checkpoint_sheet.title: Teacher Checkpoint — Final Math Recommendation
checkpoint_sheet.checkpoint_focus: Check whether final recommendations include measurement, data/probability, and cost evidence.
checkpoint_sheet.release_rule: Ready to share when the recommendation includes measurement, data/probability, and cost evidence.
